questions.question: Explain the difference between synchronous and asynchronous request-reply protocols with use cases.
keyPoints: Synchronous: Client blocks until response arrives - used in simple queries, database lookups
Asynchronous: Client continues processing, response handled via callback/promise/future - used in high-performance systems, distributed transactions
Trade-off: simplicity vs throughput/scalability

questions.question: Describe the three delivery semantics in request-reply protocols.
keyPoints: At-most-once: Request delivered at most once (may be lost), no duplicates - simplest to implement
At-least-once: Request guaranteed delivery, may have duplicates - requires retries, server must be idempotent
Exactly-once: Request executed exactly once - most desirable but hardest, requires coordination and acknowledgments

questions.question: What is RPC? Explain the steps involved in a Remote Procedure Call.
keyPoints: RPC enables executing procedures on remote machines as if local

Steps: Client calls local stub → Stub marshals parameters → Request sent over network → Server skeleton unmarshals → Actual procedure executed → Result marshalled → Response sent → Client stub unmarshals → Result returned to client

questions.question: Explain the importance of timeouts and retries in request-reply protocols.
keyPoints: Timeouts prevent infinite waiting when requests/responses lost
Timeout value must account for network latency and server processing time
Retries must consider idempotency - non-idempotent operations risk duplicate execution
Exponential backoff prevents thundering herd in distributed systems

questions.question: Compare TCP and UDP for implementing request-reply protocols.
keyPoints: TCP: Reliable, ordered, connection-oriented - guarantees delivery, higher latency due to handshake and congestion control
UDP: Unreliable, no connection overhead - lower latency, suitable for one-shot queries, requires application-level reliability
Choice depends on requirements: reliability vs latency, query complexity, expected load

questions.question: What is idempotency? Why is it important in request-reply protocols?
keyPoints: Idempotent operations produce same result regardless of execution count
Important for at-least-once delivery: safe to retry without side effects
Examples: GET, PUT, DELETE are idempotent in HTTP; POST is not
Non-idempotent operations require exactly-once semantics or careful duplicate detection

questions.question: Design a reliable request-reply protocol over UDP. Explain how you handle lost packets, duplicates, and ordering.
keyPoints: Add sequence numbers and transaction IDs to identify unique requests
Implement acknowledgments for received requests and responses
Timeout and retransmit if acknowledgment not received within expected time
Maintain sent-but-unacked requests for retransmission
Use duplicate detection at server (track processed transaction IDs)
For ordering: sequence numbers and buffering out-of-order messages
Trade-off: complexity vs reliability - document assumptions and failure modes

questions.question: Explain the architecture of RPC and discuss its advantages and limitations compared to local procedure calls.
keyPoints: RPC Architecture: Client stub, Network, Server skeleton, Server implementation
Advantages: Location transparency, Enables distributed computing, Reuse of server capabilities, Language independence (IDL)
Limitations: Network latency (cannot match local call speed), Partial failure handling, No shared memory semantics, Security boundaries, Debugging complexity
Performance considerations: Serialization overhead, Connection pooling, Async RPC

questions.question: Discuss the differences between stateless and stateful server designs in request-reply protocols. When would you choose each?
keyPoints: Stateless: No session state, each request self-contained, easy horizontal scaling, better fault tolerance
Stateful: Server maintains session info, can optimize with connection reuse, enables transactions
Stateless preferred for: Web services, microservices, high availability, load balancing scenarios
Stateful preferred for: Database connections, gaming, real-time collaboration, where context matters
Trade-offs: Stateless has more data per request, stateful has session management complexity

questions.question: Compare REST and RPC architectural styles for web services. Which would you choose for different scenarios?
keyPoints: RPC: Action-oriented, operation-based (getUser, updateOrder), tightly coupled, often uses SOAP/XML or gRPC
REST: Resource-oriented, noun-based (/users, /orders), uses HTTP methods, loose coupling, stateless
REST advantages: Caching, readability, widespread adoption, browser compatibility
RPC advantages: Fine-grained operations, better for complex actions, type-safe contracts (gRPC)
Choose REST for: CRUD operations, public APIs, caching-important scenarios
Choose RPC for: Complex business logic, real-time systems, internal microservices with performance needs
